words.definitionEn: inspiring fear, respect, or awe because of great strength, size, or ability
words.exampleEn: The chess champion was a formidable opponent who had not lost a match in three years.

words.definitionEn: happening by lucky chance rather than design
words.exampleEn: Their meeting on the train turned out to be fortuitous, since he later hired her.

words.definitionEn: irritable and difficult to control
words.exampleEn: The fractious committee could not agree on even the simplest agenda item.

words.definitionEn: not having any serious purpose or value; silly
words.exampleEn: The judge dismissed the lawsuit as frivolous, wasting the court's time.

words.definitionEn: attempting to avoid notice or attention, typically because of guilt or a desire to keep something secret
words.exampleEn: She cast a furtive glance at the door before slipping the note into her pocket.

words.definitionEn: incapable of producing any useful result; pointless
words.exampleEn: His attempts to fix the ancient printer proved futile, so he bought a new one.

words.definitionEn: excessively talkative, especially about trivial matters
words.exampleEn: The garrulous cab driver talked for the entire forty-minute ride without pausing for breath.

words.definitionEn: friendly and warm in manner
words.exampleEn: The genial host made every guest feel welcome from the moment they arrived.

words.definitionEn: relevant to a subject under consideration
words.exampleEn: The lawyer objected that the witness's comment was not germane to the case.

words.definitionEn: eating or consuming excessive amounts of food or resources
words.exampleEn: The gluttonous raccoon devoured the entire bag of trail mix in minutes.

words.definitionEn: impressively large or ambitious, often in an exaggerated or unrealistic way
words.exampleEn: His grandiose plan to build a floating city struck investors as pure fantasy.

words.definitionEn: given or done without good reason; uncalled for
words.exampleEn: Critics complained that the film's violence was gratuitous and added nothing to the story.

words.definitionEn: fond of company; sociable
words.exampleEn: Her gregarious personality made her the center of every party she attended.

words.definitionEn: clever or cunning deception used to trick someone
words.exampleEn: The con artist used guile to convince the couple to sign away their savings.

words.definitionEn: overused and lacking originality due to too much repetition
words.exampleEn: The screenwriter relied on hackneyed phrases like 'it was a dark and stormy night.'

words.definitionEn: unlucky; unfortunate
words.exampleEn: The hapless intern spilled coffee on the CEO during his very first week.

words.definitionEn: arrogantly superior and disdainful toward others
words.exampleEn: The haughty duchess refused to speak to anyone she considered beneath her rank.

words.definitionEn: a person who believes that pleasure is the most important thing in life and pursues it
words.exampleEn: As a committed hedonist, he planned every vacation around fine dining and luxury spas.

words.definitionEn: contrary to widely accepted beliefs or established doctrine
words.exampleEn: The scientist's heretical theory contradicted decades of accepted research.

words.definitionEn: a system in which people or things are ranked one above another according to status or authority
words.exampleEn: New employees quickly learned the company's strict hierarchy of approval before making any decision.

words.definitionEn: consisting of parts or elements that are all the same type
words.exampleEn: The classroom was surprisingly homogeneous, with students sharing nearly identical backgrounds.

words.definitionEn: excessive pride or self-confidence that leads to one's downfall
words.exampleEn: The general's hubris led him to ignore warnings and march his army into a trap.

words.definitionEn: lacking excitement or variety; dull and monotonous
words.exampleEn: She longed to escape her humdrum routine of commuting, working, and sleeping.

words.definitionEn: exaggerated statements not meant to be taken literally
words.exampleEn: Saying he was 'starving to death' was pure hyperbole; he had eaten just an hour earlier.

words.definitionEn: a person who attacks or challenges cherished beliefs or traditional institutions
words.exampleEn: The artist was known as an iconoclast who rejected every convention of classical painting.

words.definitionEn: a peculiar or distinctive habit or trait belonging to a particular person
words.exampleEn: One of his idiosyncrasies was refusing to eat food that touched other food on his plate.

words.definitionEn: deserving or causing public shame or disgrace
words.exampleEn: The team suffered an ignominious defeat, losing by fifty points at home.

words.definitionEn: forbidden by law, rules, or custom
words.exampleEn: Police raided the warehouse where illicit goods were being stored and sold.

words.definitionEn: unchanging over time or unable to be changed
words.exampleEn: The scientists treated the law of gravity as an immutable fact of the universe.

words.definitionEn: treating all sides or parties equally; not biased
words.exampleEn: The judge remained impartial throughout the trial, favoring neither side.

words.definitionEn: not feeling or showing emotion
words.exampleEn: He kept an impassive face even as the verdict was read aloud.

words.definitionEn: in accordance with the highest standards; flawless
words.exampleEn: Her impeccable manners impressed everyone at the formal dinner.

words.definitionEn: assuming power or authority without justification; domineering
words.exampleEn: The imperious manager barked orders at employees without ever saying please.

words.definitionEn: not showing proper respect; rude
words.exampleEn: The teacher scolded the student for his impertinent remarks during the lecture.

words.definitionEn: unable to be affected or influenced by something
words.exampleEn: The coating made the fabric impervious to water and stains.

words.definitionEn: unable to be appeased or satisfied; relentless
words.exampleEn: The detective became an implacable pursuer, never giving up on the case.

words.definitionEn: not showing due respect for another person; impertinently bold
words.exampleEn: The impudent child stuck out his tongue at the principal.

words.definitionEn: acting or done without forethought
words.exampleEn: Her impulsive decision to quit her job left her scrambling to pay rent.

words.definitionEn: not resulting from careful thought; unintentional
words.exampleEn: The chef's inadvertent use of the wrong spice ruined the entire dish.

words.definitionEn: silly; lacking sense or meaning
words.exampleEn: The talk show host asked a series of inane questions that revealed nothing new.

words.definitionEn: tending to stir up conflict; inflammatory
words.exampleEn: The politician's incendiary speech provoked outrage from both sides of the debate.

words.definitionEn: continuing without pause or interruption
words.exampleEn: The incessant rain flooded the streets after three straight days of storms.

words.definitionEn: showing clear thought and sharp understanding; direct and effective
words.exampleEn: Her incisive questions exposed the weaknesses in his argument immediately.

words.definitionEn: not in harmony or keeping with the surroundings; out of place
words.exampleEn: The skyscraper looked incongruous next to the row of centuries-old cottages.

words.definitionEn: not important; of no significant effect
words.exampleEn: He dismissed the typo as inconsequential compared to the report's overall findings.

words.definitionEn: not able to be corrected, improved, or reformed
words.exampleEn: The incorrigible prankster kept pulling jokes even after multiple detentions.

words.definitionEn: unwilling or unable to believe something
words.exampleEn: She gave an incredulous stare when told the flight had been delayed six hours.

words.definitionEn: having no particular interest or concern; showing no bias
words.exampleEn: He remained indifferent to the outcome of the game, since neither team was his favorite.

words.definitionEn: wanting to avoid activity or exertion; lazy
words.exampleEn: The indolent cat spent the entire afternoon sleeping in a sunbeam.

words.definitionEn: having or showing no skill; clumsy
words.exampleEn: His inept handling of the negotiations cost the company a major contract.

words.definitionEn: certain to happen; unavoidable
words.exampleEn: Given the storm's path, evacuation became inevitable by Tuesday morning.

words.definitionEn: well known for some bad quality or deed
words.exampleEn: The pirate became infamous for raiding ships along the entire coastline.

words.definitionEn: clever, original, and inventive
words.exampleEn: Her ingenious solution used household items to fix the broken machine.

words.definitionEn: innocent and unsuspecting; showing simple sincerity
words.exampleEn: The ingenuous child believed every word the stranger told her.

words.definitionEn: existing as a natural or basic part of something
words.exampleEn: Risk is inherent in any investment, no matter how careful the planning.

words.definitionEn: tending to obstruct or harm; hostile
words.exampleEn: The harsh climate proved inimical to farming in that region.

words.definitionEn: inborn; natural rather than acquired
words.exampleEn: She had an innate talent for music that no amount of practice could fully explain.

words.definitionEn: not harmful or offensive
words.exampleEn: The comment seemed innocuous at first, but it later caused an argument.

words.definitionEn: impossible to satisfy; always wanting more
words.exampleEn: His insatiable curiosity led him to read every book in the library.

words.definitionEn: lacking flavor, spirit, or interest; dull
words.exampleEn: The soup was so insipid that she reached for the salt shaker immediately.

words.definitionEn: showing a rude and disrespectful lack of respect
words.exampleEn: The insolent waiter rolled his eyes when asked for a refill.

words.definitionEn: narrow-minded or isolated in outlook; ignorant of other cultures
words.exampleEn: Growing up on the remote island made the community somewhat insular in its views.

words.definitionEn: to overwhelm someone with things or people to deal with
words.exampleEn: The office was inundated with complaints after the website crashed.

words.definitionEn: having a long-standing habit that is unlikely to change
words.exampleEn: As an inveterate gambler, he had lost and won several fortunes over the decades.

words.definitionEn: easily made angry; hot-tempered
words.exampleEn: The irascible chef screamed at anyone who touched his knives.

words.definitionEn: unable to decide on a course of action; hesitant
words.exampleEn: The irresolute manager kept postponing the decision, frustrating the entire team.

words.definitionEn: traveling from place to place, especially for work
words.exampleEn: The itinerant preacher moved from town to town, never staying long in one place.

words.definitionEn: fond of joking; humorous
words.exampleEn: His jocular tone put the nervous patients at ease before surgery.

words.definitionEn: having or showing good judgment; sensible
words.exampleEn: Her judicious use of the limited budget kept the project on track.

words.definitionEn: to place two things side by side for comparison or contrast
words.exampleEn: The exhibit juxtaposed old photographs with modern ones to show the city's change.

words.definitionEn: using very few words; terse
words.exampleEn: His laconic reply of 'no' ended the debate immediately.

words.definitionEn: to express sorrow or regret about something
words.exampleEn: The villagers gathered to lament the loss of their beloved schoolteacher.

words.definitionEn: lacking energy or vitality; slow and relaxed
words.exampleEn: They spent a languid afternoon swaying in hammocks by the lake.

words.definitionEn: generosity in giving money or other gifts to others
words.exampleEn: The billionaire's largesse funded a new hospital wing for the city.

words.definitionEn: present but not yet developed or manifest; hidden
words.exampleEn: The coach recognized a latent talent in the shy freshman.

words.definitionEn: deserving praise and commendation
words.exampleEn: Her laudable effort to raise funds for the shelter earned citywide recognition.

words.definitionEn: affected by sluggishness and lack of energy
words.exampleEn: After the long flight, he felt too lethargic to unpack his bags.

words.definitionEn: the treatment of a serious matter with humor or lack of due respect
words.exampleEn: She tried to bring some levity to the tense meeting with a joke.

words.definitionEn: disregarding accepted moral or sexual rules; promiscuous
words.exampleEn: The novel's licentious characters shocked readers accustomed to more modest fiction.

words.definitionEn: very talkative
words.exampleEn: The loquacious guide narrated every detail of the tour, whether asked or not.

words.definitionEn: clear and easy to understand; mentally clear
words.exampleEn: Despite her illness, the patient remained lucid enough to answer every question.

words.definitionEn: producing a great deal of profit
words.exampleEn: The startup's founders signed a lucrative deal with a major retailer.

words.definitionEn: so foolish, unreasonable, or out of place as to be amusing
words.exampleEn: The idea of driving across the country in a golf cart seemed ludicrous.

words.definitionEn: a general feeling of discomfort, illness, or unease
words.exampleEn: A sense of malaise spread through the office after the layoffs were announced.

words.definitionEn: having or showing a wish to do evil to others
words.exampleEn: The malevolent villain plotted to destroy the entire village out of spite.

words.definitionEn: to exaggerate or fake illness to avoid work or duty
words.exampleEn: The soldier was accused of malingering to avoid being sent to the front lines.

words.definitionEn: easily influenced or shaped; capable of being molded
words.exampleEn: Gold is a highly malleable metal, easily hammered into thin sheets.

words.definitionEn: lying; untruthful
words.exampleEn: The witness's mendacious testimony was later contradicted by video evidence.

words.definitionEn: motivated primarily by desire for money or personal gain
words.exampleEn: His mercenary approach to charity work made colleagues question his true motives.

words.definitionEn: subject to sudden or unpredictable changes of mood
words.exampleEn: The mercurial director could praise an actor one moment and scream at them the next.

words.definitionEn: deserving reward or praise
words.exampleEn: The soldier received a medal for his meritorious service during the rescue mission.

words.definitionEn: a complete change of form or nature
words.exampleEn: The caterpillar's metamorphosis into a butterfly fascinated the young students.

words.definitionEn: a person who dislikes and avoids other people
words.exampleEn: The old misanthrope lived alone in the woods and refused all visitors.

words.definitionEn: to calm or soothe an angry or upset person
words.exampleEn: The manager tried to mollify the furious customer with a full refund.

words.definitionEn: sullen and ill-tempered; gloomy
words.exampleEn: He sat in the corner, morose and silent, after losing the championship.

words.definitionEn: lacking excitement; dull; commonplace
words.exampleEn: Washing dishes and folding laundry are among life's most mundane chores.

words.definitionEn: extremely generous
words.exampleEn: The munificent donor gave enough to build an entire new library wing.

words.definitionEn: lacking imagination, foresight, or intellectual insight; narrow-minded
words.exampleEn: Critics called the policy myopic, warning it ignored long-term consequences.

words.definitionEn: wicked or criminal
words.exampleEn: The detective uncovered the crime boss's nefarious scheme just in time.

words.definitionEn: failing to take proper care over something; careless
words.exampleEn: The company was found negligent after ignoring repeated safety warnings.

quizzes.passage: Unlike her introverted brother, who preferred solitary hikes, Mia was gregarious, organizing weekly game nights and always the first to introduce herself to new neighbors.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'gregarious' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The contrast with her 'introverted brother' and her habit of organizing game nights shows Mia's outgoing, sociable nature.

quizzes.passage: As a self-described hedonist, Marco structured his entire year around pleasure: leisurely brunches, spa weekends, and vacations chosen solely for their beaches and cuisine.
quizzes.question: Which choice best describes the function of the phrase 'structured his entire year around pleasure' in the passage?

options.t: It proves Marco is financially irresponsible.
It contradicts the claim that Marco is a hedonist.
It illustrates what makes Marco a hedonist.
It shows Marco's disdain for luxury.

quizzes.explanation: The phrase provides concrete evidence — brunches, spas, vacations chosen for pleasure — supporting the label 'hedonist.'

quizzes.passage: The founders wrote the amendment process to be difficult, but they never intended the Constitution's core principles to be treated as immutable; they expected future generations to adapt them.
quizzes.question: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

options.t: The amendment process was designed to be effortless.
The Constitution cannot be changed under any circumstances.
Future generations rejected the founders' intentions.
The founders expected the Constitution's principles to evolve, not remain fixed forever.

quizzes.explanation: The passage states the founders 'never intended' the principles to be unchangeable and expected adaptation, which is the main idea.

quizzes.passage: Amid the funeral's solemn atmosphere, a moment of levity arrived when the grandfather's favorite silly joke was read aloud, and mourners laughed through their tears.
quizzes.question: Which choice best describes the function of the phrase 'a moment of levity' in the passage?

options.t: It proves the joke was inappropriate.
It contradicts the description of the funeral as solemn.
It highlights a brief break from the solemn mood through humor.
It shows that the mourners were disrespectful.

quizzes.explanation: The phrase marks a shift from solemnity to a brief, humorous moment, which is the function of levity in the passage.

quizzes.passage: Though the visiting chess grandmaster looked unassuming in his rumpled sweater, local players quickly learned he was a formidable opponent: in twenty simultaneous games, he lost not a single one, dispatching challengers with ruthless efficiency.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'formidable' most nearly means

options.t: impressively powerful and hard to defeat

quizzes.explanation: The passage describes the grandmaster winning all twenty games with ruthless efficiency, showing he was powerful and difficult to beat.

quizzes.passage: By the third hour of the budget meeting, the once-orderly committee had become fractious, members interrupting one another and refusing to compromise on even the smallest line item.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'fractious' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Members interrupting each other and refusing to compromise shows the committee had become irritable and difficult to control.

quizzes.passage: Engineers spent months trying to seal the leak, but every patch failed within days; eventually they admitted their efforts were futile and redesigned the entire pipeline from scratch.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'futile' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Every patch failing within days shows the engineers' repair efforts produced no useful result.

quizzes.passage: Unlike the stiff, formal professors down the hall, Dr. Alvarez had a genial way of greeting every student by name and asking about their weekend before diving into calculus.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'genial' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Greeting students warmly and asking about their weekend contrasts with 'stiff, formal' professors, showing friendliness.

quizzes.passage: The novel's final chapter includes a gratuitous ten-page description of a battle that has no bearing on the plot, seemingly included only to pad the page count.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'gratuitous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The description 'has no bearing on the plot' and seems included only to pad length, meaning it was unnecessary.

quizzes.passage: The critic panned the romance novel for its hackneyed plot: star-crossed lovers, a jealous rival, and a wedding interrupted at the last second — tropes readers had seen a thousand times before.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'hackneyed' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The critic notes readers 'had seen a thousand times before,' meaning the plot elements were overused clichés.

quizzes.passage: When the young monk proposed that scripture be reinterpreted in light of new discoveries, church elders branded the idea heretical and banned him from preaching.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'heretical' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The elders' reaction — banning the monk — shows his proposal was seen as violating established religious doctrine.

quizzes.passage: The suburb's population was remarkably homogeneous: nearly every household shared the same income bracket, the same commute, and the same taste in lawn ornaments.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'homogeneous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Sharing the same income, commute, and taste across nearly every household describes a population that is uniform, not diverse.

quizzes.passage: Rejecting the conservative academy's rules of proportion and realism, the young sculptor became an iconoclast, smashing traditional forms to create jagged, unrecognizable shapes that scandalized critics.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'iconoclast' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Rejecting the academy's rules and scandalizing critics with unconventional forms marks the sculptor as someone who challenges tradition.

quizzes.passage: After being caught bribing officials, the mayor resigned in an ignominious ceremony, escorted from city hall past reporters shouting questions about the scandal.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'ignominious' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Resigning after a bribery scandal while reporters shout questions describes a public and disgraceful exit.

quizzes.passage: Even as the crowd erupted in cheers and jeers around him, the veteran umpire remained impassive, his expression unchanged as he made the final call.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'impassive' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The umpire's expression stayed unchanged despite the crowd's reaction, showing an absence of visible emotion.

quizzes.passage: The new supervisor's imperious manner — issuing commands without explanation and expecting instant obedience — quickly alienated a staff accustomed to collaborative decision-making.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'imperious' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Issuing commands without explanation and demanding instant obedience describes domineering, authoritarian behavior.

quizzes.passage: No apology, no compromise, and no plea could satisfy the implacable creditor, who pursued the family for every last cent regardless of their circumstances.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'implacable' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: No apology or plea could satisfy the creditor, showing he could not be appeased or softened.

quizzes.passage: When told the marathon had been canceled due to a parade permit mix-up, the runners stared back with incredulous expressions, certain they had misheard.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'incredulous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Being 'certain they had misheard' shows the runners could not believe the news, matching 'incredulous.'

quizzes.passage: The editor cut the subplot, deeming it inconsequential to a story that was, at its heart, about a father reconciling with his estranged son.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'inconsequential' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The editor cut the subplot because it did not matter to the core story, meaning it was unimportant.

quizzes.passage: The indolent teenager spent the entire summer on the couch, ignoring both his chores and the pile of college applications on his desk.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'indolent' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Spending the whole summer on the couch while ignoring responsibilities is a clear illustration of laziness.

quizzes.passage: The city's infamous bridge collapse of 1907 is still studied by engineering students as a case of catastrophic design failure.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'infamous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Being remembered as a 'catastrophic design failure' shows the bridge collapse is notorious, not admired.

quizzes.passage: Trusting every stranger's story without question, the ingenuous traveler handed over his passport to a man who claimed to be a hotel employee.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'ingenuous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Trusting a stranger's unverified claim and handing over a passport shows naive, unsuspecting trust.

quizzes.passage: The mild rash turned out to be innocuous, caused by nothing more serious than an allergic reaction to a new detergent.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'innocuous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The rash's cause was minor and not serious, meaning it was harmless.

quizzes.passage: The critics called the film's dialogue insipid, complaining that every line felt flat and forgettable next to the stunning visuals.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'insipid' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Dialogue described as 'flat and forgettable' is dull and lacking in interest, matching 'insipid.'

quizzes.passage: After the celebrity's tweet went viral, her inbox was inundated with thousands of messages within minutes, crashing the app's servers.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'inundated' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Receiving thousands of messages within minutes, enough to crash servers, is being overwhelmed by volume.

quizzes.passage: The irascible landlord exploded over a single late rent payment, shouting threats before the tenant could even finish her apology.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'irascible' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Exploding with shouted threats over one late payment shows the landlord was quick-tempered.

quizzes.passage: Even during the tense negotiation, the diplomat kept a jocular tone, cracking small jokes that eased the room without undermining the seriousness of the talks.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'jocular' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Cracking small jokes to ease tension is a display of playful humor, matching 'jocular.'

quizzes.passage: The curator chose to juxtapose a Renaissance oil painting with a digital projection, inviting visitors to compare five centuries of artistic technique in a single glance.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'juxtapose' most nearly means

options.t: to place side by side for comparison

quizzes.explanation: Placing the two artworks together so visitors could compare them directly is the definition of juxtaposing.

quizzes.passage: The languid pace of the tropical afternoon — slow-moving fans, half-finished naps, unhurried conversation — stood in sharp contrast to the frantic energy of the city she had left.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'languid' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Slow-moving fans, half-finished naps, and unhurried conversation all point to a relaxed, low-energy pace.

quizzes.passage: The gene remained latent for three generations, producing no visible effect until an environmental trigger finally activated it in the youngest grandchild.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'latent' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The gene existed for generations without producing effects until triggered, showing it was present but dormant.

quizzes.passage: The retired diplomat, known for being loquacious at dinner parties, could turn a simple question about the weather into a forty-minute story about his travels.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'loquacious' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Turning a simple weather question into a forty-minute story shows extreme talkativeness.

quizzes.passage: The idea of shipping ice cream across the desert by camel struck the investors as ludicrous, and they laughed the pitch out of the room.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'ludicrous' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The investors laughing the pitch out of the room confirms the idea was seen as absurd.

quizzes.passage: Suspecting the employee was malingering, the manager noted that his 'illness' always coincided with major holidays and never with a doctor's note.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'malingering' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Illness always coinciding with holidays and lacking a doctor's note suggests the employee was faking sickness.

quizzes.passage: The mercenary contractor cared nothing for the community's needs, choosing every material based solely on which supplier offered him the largest kickback.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'mercenary' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Choosing materials solely for the largest kickback shows the contractor was driven purely by financial gain.

quizzes.passage: Known throughout the village as a misanthrope, old Mr. Kettner boarded up his windows and posted a sign reading 'No visitors, ever' at the edge of his property.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'misanthrope' most nearly means

options.t: one who dislikes people and avoids company

quizzes.explanation: Boarding up windows and posting a sign refusing visitors shows a strong avoidance of other people.

quizzes.passage: After losing the scholarship, he grew morose, skipping meals with friends and answering every question with a one-word, gloomy reply.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'morose' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Skipping meals with friends and giving one-word gloomy replies illustrates a sullen, depressed mood.

quizzes.passage: Critics accused the mayor of myopic planning, noting that the new highway would ease traffic for five years but ignored the city's long-term population growth.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'myopic' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Solving only a five-year problem while ignoring long-term growth is a lack of foresight, or myopic planning.

quizzes.passage: The daycare was shut down after inspectors found the staff negligent, leaving toddlers unsupervised near an unlocked pool for hours.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'negligent' most nearly means

options.t: careless and failing in one's duty

quizzes.explanation: Leaving toddlers unsupervised near an unlocked pool is a clear failure of duty, matching 'negligent.'
